a1-h1-q1: Which frame geometry should I choose?
a1-h1-q2: I sought to combine the geometry of a road bike with that of a gravel bike, whilst also being able to adapt it to that of a touring bike.
a1-h1-s1-t1: Inspired by the designs of Cyfac, I determined the angles based on their technical specifications.
a1-h1-s1-t2: Decisions informed by framebuilders' operator manuals to anticipate the impact of frame tubes angles on behaviour.
a1-h1-s1-t3: A slightly raked top tube and low seat stays for responsiveness, featuring sliding "Rocker" dropouts.
a1-h1-s1-t4: This addition allows you toadjust the wheel size to suit your needs. The option to use a 700C carbon fork (road/gravel) or a 650B steel fork (touring) ensures that the bike’s handling remains unchanged.
a1-h1-s2-l5: an easy to weld material , which ensures good serviceability.
a1-h1-s2-p1: I selected Columbus tubes and adjusted the wall thicknesses, cross-sections and steel grades to achieve the desired performance.
a1-h1-s2-q2: For the tubes, I opted for the strength and reliability of high-grade Columbus steel.
a1-h1-s2-t1: High-grade steel offers ideal mechanical properties for:
a1-h1-s2-t2: Next, definition of standards: drivetrain, wheel axles, bottom bracket, headset, braking, cable routing and harness for the dynamo hub.
a1-h1-s2-t3: Also: threaded mounts for accessories, rear triangle opening for belt drive, and maximum tyre width varying between 700C (34c) and 650B (42c) wheel setups.
a1-h2-q1: A project supervised by an industry expert!
a1-h2-q2: An internship as a framebuilder apprentice at LaFraise in Roubaix, under the supervision of Andréa, a craftsman renowned for the quality of his work.
a1-h2-t1: I benefited from his expertise and a fully equipped workshop: workbench, soldering station, files, angle measuring tools, dies, consumables and a stock of high-performance tubes.
a1-h2-t2: This workshop is in high demand, and places need to be booked more than a year in advance. I have used this time to gain a deeper understanding of the theory behind bronze and silver brazing, as well as to refine my technical choices.
a1-h2-t3: Once there, we double-checked the measurements and adjusted the geometry. Andréa taught me how to braze (flame adjustment, temperatures, technique), then I cut, notched, filed and adjusted the tubes before tack welding and final assembly.
a1-h3-q2: I am planning to acquire the necessary equipment for my next project: building a cargo bike frame , therefore exploring new applications of engineering in cycling.
a1-h3-t1: Understanding and selecting materials, learning brazing techniques: every step has deepened my understanding of frame-building. The internship at LaFraise has grounded my engineering skills in a practical context and highlighted the value of craftsmanship.
a1-h3-t2: Next step: painting the frame, the finishing touch which will be done by myself, of course!
a1-intro: Designing and building a bespoke bicycle frame from scratch, designed for versatility: agile and fast in summer, stable and reliable in winter. I oversaw the entire process, from the frame geometry to the technical choices (materials, tubes, drivetrain and braking standards), right through to the manufacturing: tube notching, fitting, tack welding and assembly using bronze and silver brazing.
a1-tile-intro: Build a made-to-measure, versatile, high-performance bicycle frame from A to Z, under the supervision of a recognised craftsman.
a2-h1: A collection as a technological showcase
a2-h1-t1: The frame is a platform for innovation, thanks to its materials (steel, aluminium, titanium, composites) and its assembly processes (welding, press-fitting, casting, and more recently additive manufacturing). Techniques such as butting steel tubes, or the evolution of geometries based on biomechanics and wind tunnel testing, illustrate these advances.
a2-h1-t2: The components have evolved accordingly: from the original fixed gear to the latest wireless electronic derailleurs, and from fragile wooden rims to aerodynamic, tubeless carbon rims.
a2-h1-t3: Each bike thus becomes a showcase of the technology of its time, and helps me to link technical choices with performance.
a2-h2: A collection that elevates craftsmanship to the level of art
a2-h2-t1: Second dimension: craftsmanship as an art form. Master frame builders such as Masi, Colnago, Cinelli (Italy), Routens and Sablière (France) or Samson (Japan) have crafted unique “pieces”, at the cutting edge of their time.
a2-h2-t2: Certain components, such as Campagnolo groupset parts, some of which are hand-engraved, and various interpretations of aerodynamics add to this aesthetic appeal.
a2-h2-t3: Paintjobs matter too: artists such as Mario Martini (Somec) have turned the frame into a canvas in its own right.
a2-h3: A collection of historic sporting achievements
a2-h3-t1: Third aspect: history. Several bicycles bear witness to notable sporting achievements.
a2-h3-t2: Among them: the bike ridden by Marc Sergeant, stage winner at the 1987 Tour de France (Lotto-Eddy Merckx); that ofAlain Bondue, silver medallist at the 1980 Moscow Olympics; and an exact replica of the time trial bike ridden by Laurent Fignon in the 1989 Tour, which he lost by 8 seconds to Greg LeMond.
a2-h4-t1: Overall, this collection offers both a technical and historical exploration of cycling. Each bicycle in the collection bears witness to the advances in engineering, design and craftsmanship characteristic of its era. Beyond the mere assembly of components, each one represents a milestone in the evolution of the sport and helps to document its history, whilst preserving the sporting heritage associated with it.
a2-intro: My collection of bicycles bears witness to the technical advances in cycling, to craftsmanship that borders on art, and to great sporting achievements. Each bicycle, carefully selected, tells a story.
a2-tile-intro: Reverse engineering to understand the evolution of technology in cycling.
a2-title: A passion for cycling and a historical collection
a3-h1-t1: Having grown up surrounded by photography, I took an interest in it at an early age. I moved on from digital and smartphone photography tofilm photography– a more demanding medium – and deepened my understanding of the fundamentals of exposure by reading specialist literature.
a3-h2: A collection combining reverse engineering and history
a3-h2-t1: I am interested in the evolution of photographic equipment, from the first full-frame cameras developed by Oskar Barnack in 1913 to the Hasselblad models adapted for the Apollo missions. This passion has led me to collect film cameras and to study how they work through reverse engineering. Iconic models such as the Contax II, used to document racial segregation, or the Nikon F, employed by many photojournalists during the Vietnam War, illustrate both the technical advances of their era and their role in history. This exploration allows me to better understand the evolution of photography and to put these innovations into perspective with the digital technologies I use today in a semi-professional capacity.
a3-h3-t1: Coming from a family with a long tradition of photography, I have come to see this art form as a means of expression that is both personal and technical. My interest in film cameras led me to study how they work through reverse engineering, thereby deepening my understanding of their evolution and their role in documenting historical events. Through collecting iconic models and analysing their mechanisms, I have developed both a historical understanding and technical skills. Today, I wish to continue this approach by honing my skills in film printing and sharing this work at an upcoming exhibition.
a3-intro: For me, photography is a means of expression as much as it is a way of capturing reality. I work with both digital and film photography, and I’m interested in the history and evolution of cameras.
a3-tile-intro: Using photography as a means of expression and collecting cameras with a rich technological history.
a3-title: Semi-pro photography and analog camera collection
a4-h1-t1: In the field of additive manufacturing, I have upgraded my first 3D printer (2016) through various modifications (custom firmware, auto-levelling sensors, heated enclosure) and explored printing parameters as well as a wide range of engineering materials. Almost ten years on, the machines I use today have become almost entirely plug-and-play, illustrating the progress made in this field.
a4-h1-t2: At the same time, I have been involved in projects combining electronics, mechanics and audio: building open-source ergonomic keyboards (Ergodox), repairing electronic devices, restoring watches and designing loudspeakers. These projects have led me to carry out a wide range of tasks, from replacing components and SMD soldering to the complete assembly of computers and the modification of embedded systems.
a4-h1-t3: Some examples of our work: replacement of screens, batteries and flex cables; soldering of SMD components onto motherboards; complete computer assembly (dual boot, overclocking, thermal paste); rooting of phones; updating custom firmware and enabling features on automotive MMI units.
a4-h1-t4: I am also interested in interior design and renovation. This passion led me to design and carry out the complete renovation of an 80 m² flat, handling everything from the layout of the spaces to the structural work, finishes, surfacing and electrical installation. I also carry out all routine maintenance on my vehicles that does not require highly specialised tools. More than just a hobby, this is a way for me to learn about, understand and master the systems around me.
a4-h2-t1: From upgrading my 3D printers to repairing electronics, as well as making objects, working with machinery and doing manual tasks, every project is an opportunity to explore how the systems around me work. Inherited from a family environment characterised by technical curiosity, this maker DIY culture has become a genuine method of learning and experimentation in my daily life.
a4-intro: The maker culture is part of who I am, inherited from a family tradition characterised by a passion for technology: a great-grandfather who designed wind turbines, a grandfather who was an entrepreneur in the aerospace industry, and a father involved in the Ariane space programme. It has fuelled my passion for understanding and exploring the systems around me.
a4-tile-intro: Explore a range of areas within the maker (DIY) culture: additive manufacturing, electronics, general mechanics and repairs.
a5-h1-t1: Cycling and hiking are my favourite physical challenges. Every ride—whether on the road, gravel or cross-country—is an adventure; and multi-day hikes, from mountain hut to mountain hut, bring me a real sense of peace. For me, nothing beats the simplicity of the great outdoors.
a5-h1-t2: Having grown up in Annecy, downhill skiing comes naturally to me and is a real passion. Having now recovered from a cruciate ligament injury, I’m back on my skis and enjoying it just as much as ever.
a5-h2-t1: Outside of sport, I am interested in the social sciences (anthropology, psychology, sociology) and topics such as the ethics of innovation, power dynamics and the impact of technology, including artificial intelligence. Nature and biodiversity also fuel my curiosity, as does philosophical reflection.
a5-h3-t1: These activities satisfy my insatiable curiosity and provide me with the balance I need between physical exertion, reflection and contemplation. It’s a way of constantly broadening my horizons.
a5-intro: I find fulfilment in both physical activity and intellectual pursuits: mountain hiking, cycling and skiing on the one hand, and social sciences and philosophy on the other. It’s all part of the same quest for balance between body and mind.
a5-tile-intro: Explore the mountain trails on foot, by bike or on skis.
a6-c1-f1: 1. Selection of the operating point for starting the generator.
a6-c1-f2: 2. Effect of magnetic field concentrators on LHT.
a6-c1-f3: 3. Physical phenomena involved and analytical study.
a6-c1-f4: 4. Protocol and objectives of the trial campaign.
a6-c2-f5: 5. Example of a part after LHT.
a6-c2-f6: 6. Inducer used for this test campaign.
a6-c2-f7: 7. Cutting the parts to examine the actual hardening profile.
a6-c2-f8: 8. Taguchi table of the configurations studied.
a6-h1-p1: The process involves a short heating cycle followed by rapid quenching , which locks the microstructure.
a6-h1-p2: Long based on empirical methods, its development now relies on finite element simulation.
a6-h1-p3: The simulation aims to determine the shape of the inductor and the control of the generator in order to achieve the quenching profile required for the product.
a6-h1-p4: Setting up the data requires specifying a number of parameters: the part’s mesh, control of the high-frequency generator, and data transfers between solvers.
a6-h1-p5: To improve the process, I have implemented an R&D strategy capable of analysing a wide range of factors.
a6-h2-l1:
Understanding the behaviour of the software by analysing the influence of the input parameters.
In particular, using steel test specimens treated with an inductor made from sintered copper powder.
a6-h2-l2: Conduct a sensitivity analysis using a design of experiments, implemented as a series of tests.
a6-h2-l3: Define the configurations to be tested using Taguchi’s design of experiments method.
a6-h2-l4: Analyse the results against the simulations, then send my conclusions to the publisher for the calibration of the solvers.
a6-h2-l5:
Equipping the LHT department.
Creating Excel spreadsheets using VBA to determine optimal operating points, and drafting a best-practice guide.
a6-h2-q2: Data entry is made easier, and the LHT development cycle becomes more efficient and applicable to new products.
a6-h4-t1: This project involved several disciplines: mechanics, thermodynamics, electromagnetism and materials science. By comparing the simulated hardening profiles with the test results, I was able to calibrate the process to meet the specifications as closely as possible — and gauge the extent to which linking theory and practice drives industrial optimisation.
a6-h4-t2: The design of experiments identified the factors that actually influence the process, transforming an empirical development process into a controlled approach. Supporting the LHT teams and collaborating with the software publisher reminded me that methodological rigour only bears fruit if it is passed on: communication is part of the result.
a6-h4-t3: All in all, a shorter and more consistent LHT development cycle for new products and, for me, a project that has strengthened my ability to lead industrial R&D at the intersection of several disciplines.
a6-intro: NTN-SNR Bearings produces more than 500,000 bearings every day for a wide range of industrial sectors. Their raceways are hardened using localised induction heat treatment (LHT), which stabilises the austenitic structure of the steel to extend the bearing’s service life. My role: to optimise this process to ensure consistent quality, reduce cycle times and accelerate its implementation in new products.
a6-tile-intro: Optimise a heat treatment process to ensure compliance with specifications, reduce development times and increase production rates.
a6-title: Optimisation of localised induction heat treatment
a7-c1-f1: 1. Specific solution requiring fine adjustment (wear) and the intervention of an operator during blocking.
a7-c1-f2: 2. Solution deployed on a production line.
a7-c1-f3: 3. Adaptable solution requiring very fine adjustment of the rollers, with insufficient robustness.
a7-c1-f4: 4. Previous studies have not led to the development of a satisfactory solution.
a7-c2-f5: 5. The use of a Keyence laser beam sensor is at the heart of the proposed solution.
a7-c2-f6: 6. Development of electro-pneumatic circuits, control program and other components.
a7-c2-f8: 8. A multitude of components are needed for the prototype to work properly.
a7-h0-p0:
Incorrectly aligned rollers were passing between the finishing and superfinishing machines, causing:
  • damage to the grinding wheels and a costly production stoppage;
  • a jam requiring time-consuming manual intervention;
  • a line restart that compromised quality and generated significant scrap.
a7-h1-t1:
Develop a transit module that monitors the orientation of conical rolls as they are transferred between the finishing and superfinishing stages.

Reliable and robust, it had to detect incorrect orientation at high speed (350 rollers/min) and then eject the roller automatically, without human intervention and without disrupting production.
a7-h2-p0: There were nine key stages throughout the mission.
a7-h2-p1: Analysis of the current situation : to identify the solutions currently in place in order to assess their performance and limitations.
a7-h2-p2-q1: I chose a Keyence laser beam sensor for its precision and responsiveness, which is suitable for high production rates (up to 350 rolls/min) and small diameter variations (less than 3 mm difference between the diameters of the faces).
a7-h2-p3: Definition of the machine's kinematics , followed by the geometric design of the components.
a7-h2-p3-q1: I have taken care to design a machine that is easy to adjust, resistant to wear and tear, compatible with a range of models , and which does not damage the treated rolls..
a7-h2-p4-q1: Electrical power and control circuit for a stepper motor and its driver, electro-pneumatic circuit for a cylinder, and management of the machine’s optical laser sensors.
a7-h2-p6: Wiring the inputs/outputs on the Arduino board and programming the control system in C.
a7-h2-p7: Fine-tuning : a series of tests to adjust the control system and the geometry of certain components, sometimes by adding sub-assemblies to improve the reliability of the sorting process. The use of a high-speed camera enabled the machine’s operation to be fine-tuned through close observation of the processed rolls.
a7-h2-p8: Real-world trials on production lines to validate the process.
a7-h2-p9: Drafting a report to be sent to the department responsible for rolling out the solution on the other lines.
a7-h3-p1: Based on a high-precision laser sensor, the solution developed ensures effective monitoring of high-speed rollers with minimal impact on the rollers. The design, manufacturing and optimisation phases enabled us to meet the requirements specified by the internal client, which were validated during tests under real-world conditions.
a7-h3-p2: This project was a particularly formative experience. It gave me the opportunity to lead an innovation project from start to finish, from defining the requirement through to implementation, and to measure its operational benefits in concrete terms.
a7-h3-q1: Reliability checks completed; new process validated.
a7-h3-q2: The solution made it possible to monitor the rollers at high speed whilst minimising wear and waste. It was a formative project that allowed me to combine technical design, hands-on experimentation and driving innovation.
a7-intro: NTN-SNR manufactures all the components of a bearing (rings, rolling elements and cages), including tapered rollers. My role: to develop an autonomous system to monitor their orientation in order to eliminate costly production line stoppages. I led the project from start to finish: analysis of the existing system, selection of technologies, followed by mechanical, electro-pneumatic and embedded design, development and final validation.
a7-tile-intro: Design a high-speed autonomous inspection machine to reduce downtime on production lines.
a7-title: Development of an industrial handling module
a8-c1-f1: 4. Few studies are available on the stresses on the cage (ADORE simulation).
a8-c1-f3: 3. Topological optimisation process and redesign of a part.
a8-c2-f4: 5. Simulation of stresses at the ball-cage contact.
a8-c2-f6: 7. Simulation of the stresses experienced by one of the proposed geometries.
a8-c2-f7: 8. Example of a geometry obtained following redesign.
a8-c2-f8: 9. Determination of the maximum cage volume to be specified for the study.
a8-c2-f9: 10. Example of an unsatisfactory result due to incorrect modelling parameters.
a8-h0-p1: Metal additive manufacturing (MAM) opens up new possibilities in mechanical design. It allows for the creation of novel geometries by freeing itself from the constraints imposed by traditional manufacturing processes.
a8-h0-p2: The aim was therefore to demonstrate the innovative potential of MAM in improving bearings, in order to encourage future R&D investment in this technology.
a8-h1-t1: My aim was to produce a prototype that fully exploited the potential offered by MAM in the context of bearing improvement.
a8-h1-t2: With this in mind, the aim was to redesign the housing of an aerospace bearing in order to enhance its functionality whilst reducing its weight.
a8-h1-t3: One of the applications explored concerned an engine bearing whose active lubrication remains vulnerable to a circuit interruption. The stopping of the pumps can then cause overheating leading to bearing failure and, in extreme cases, engine failure.
a8-h2-h1: 1 - Analysis of the behaviour of lubricated aircraft bearings
a8-h2-h1-p1: I began by reviewing the company’s internal literature to identify studies on the mechanical behaviour of these bearings. The aim was to understand the forces acting on the cage, the stresses experienced during operation, and the challenges associated with lubrication.
a8-h2-h1-p2: This study revealed that the available data was limited, largely due to the difficulty of fitting instruments to a cage whilst it is in operation. It also highlighted scope for optimisation, as existing designs are largely oversized as a precautionary measure.
a8-h2-h1-q1: A mature field, but one that still offers plenty of scope for improvement.
a8-h2-h2-p1: I then taught myself how to use the SolidWorks topological optimisation FE module before carrying out a series of finite element simulations aimed at reducing the weight whilst maintaining the required mechanical performance.
a8-h2-h2-p2: The results identified areas suitable for the incorporation of grooves and channels capable of storing and then redistributing the lubricant in the event of a temporary interruption to the supply.
a8-h2-h3-p1: The selected concepts were produced as plastic-printed prototypes in order to assess their feasibility and select the most promising solution prior to final production by a specialist subcontractor. I then summarised the approach, the design choices and the potential benefits in a presentation document submitted to the client, who approved the proposed solution.
a8-h2-t1: The project was divided into three phases:
a8-h3-q1-p1: The MAM’s potential has been demonstrated.
a8-h3-q1-p2: Research has shown that metal additive manufacturing makes it possible to enhance the functionality of a bearing cage whilst reducing its weight, thanks to geometries that cannot be produced using conventional machining.
a8-h3-t1: This demonstration confirmed the validity of the approach and helped to generate interest among internal stakeholders in the development of this technology. It shows that it is still possible to open up new avenues in an industrial sector that is otherwise considered to be highly mature.
a8-h3-t2: This project also enabled me to develop my skills in topological optimisation, computer-aided design and the use of innovative manufacturing technologies applied to mechanical engineering.
a8-intro: Although NTN-SNR Roulements has been recognised for over a century as a major player in the manufacture of bearings, the company continues to explore new avenues of innovation. As part of this, I led a project aimed at demonstrating the potential of metal additive manufacturing (MAM) for the redesign of aerospace products. The aim was to redesign a bearing cage to enhance its functionality and reduce its weight by leveraging the unprecedented possibilities offered by this process, whilst producing a demonstrator capable of generating interest for future R&D investment.
a8-tile-intro: Exploring the potential of metal additive manufacturing to enhance the functionality of an aerospace bearing.
a8-title: Redesign of an aerospace bearing cage
a9-c1-f1: 1. Example of a solution proposed by a competing start-up.
a9-c1-f2: 2. Waterfall project management method in several distinct phases.

a9-c1-f4: 4. Application of the VPD method to assess the Product Market Fit of the solution developed.
a9-c1-f5: 5. Definition of a precise business model to assess the viability of the project and its constraints.
a9-c2-f6: 6. Use of the Figma "FigJam" tool for effective remote collaboration.

a9-c2-f9: 9. Use of Slack to communicate about the project.

a9-c3-f12: 12. Technical documentation for a partner service integrated into the solution.
a9-c4-f14: 14. Sitemap architecture diagram of the solution.
a9-c4-f15: 15. Example of UX/UI elements for prototyping.
a9-h0-p1: For this project, I worked with a team to develop a B2B SaaS platform for film distributors.
a9-h0-p2: The initial idea was to make their jobs easier by automating their repetitive day-to-day tasks. As there were no truly reliable tools available in this sector, we began by gathering and analysing their needs, as well as the ways in which they collaborate with the various players in the film distribution sector.
a9-h1-p1: As with any entrepreneurial venture, I wasn’t confined to a single role. I played a pivotal role linking product, business and technical teams.
a9-h1-s1-t1: Market analysis : studying trends and identifying unmet needs.
a9-h1-s1-t2: Value Proposition Design : defining and validating the value proposition.
a9-h1-s1-t3: Business strategy : developing market entry strategies, modelling profitability and financial requirements.
a9-h1-s2-q1: Non-technical staff often have a poor understanding of the constraints and requirements of software development. A part of my role was to clarify these issues at every stage of the design and development process.
a9-h1-s2-t1: Implementation ofsuitable collaborative tools to facilitate communication and teamwork.
a9-h1-s2-t2: Facilitating collaboration between members of the product and technical teams.
a9-h1-s3-q1: It was necessary to remain flexible regarding the definition of functional objects so that they could be adapted in line with future user feedback.
a9-h1-s3-t1: Business requirements : understanding needs and translating them into functional specifications.
a9-h1-s3-t2: Technical constraints : analysis of limitations and their implications for the design.
a9-h1-s3-t3: Technology choices : contributing to the definition of the technical stack and data models.
a9-h1-s4-t1: UX/UI design : learning the principles of user experience and interface design.
a9-h1-s4-t2: Prototyping : designing wireframes and mock-ups for the web application.
a9-h3-q1: The recent launch of a similar solution by a major player in the sector has, however, confirmed the validity of the need that was initially identified.
a9-h3-q2: Coming to terms with the cancellation of a project that was close to my heart was a significant milestone in my journey, prompting me to take a step back and reconsider the notion of failure.
a9-h3-t1: Unfortunately, the project had to be halted due to a lack of funds and technical obstacles that required external partnerships which were difficult to establish.
a9-h3-t2: This project was a particularly formative experience, enabling me to develop my versatility and gain a better understanding of the challenges involved in communication, management and strategy.
a9-h3-t3: Knowing when to call a project off is essential. I have learnt to maintain a critical perspective at all times to avoid leading a team down a dead end.
a9-intro: The film distribution industry is a complex sector, characterised by highly fragmented distribution channels and difficulties in effectively reaching target audiences. Against this backdrop, we set out to design a software solution tailored to the needs of distributors. I assembled a team comprising industry professionals and developers, and played a pivotal role covering product design, organisation, sales and operational oversight.
a9-tile-intro: Leading a B2B SaaS start-up project for film distribution, from identifying customer needs to developing a prototype.
education-intro: The path that laid the foundations for my career as a mechanical engineer, from my DUT to INSA, complemented by a deliberate move into software at École 42 to better design products combining mechanics, electronics and embedded systems.
education-text-1: INSA Lyon is a renowned engineering school, whose Mechanical Engineering department is ranked among the best in France. I studied Product Design and Innovation there on a work-study programme, combining high-level teaching with continuous industrial immersion at NTN-SNR: design, simulation and industrialisation of mechanical systems.
education-text-2: Following my experience in mechanical R&D, I made a conscious decision to broaden my horizons by joining École 42 in Paris, a leading institution for software development training founded by Xavier Niel. Its project-based teaching method, with no lectures or teachers, is based on self-directed learning and collaborative problem-solving; there, I gained a solid grounding in software architecture and low-level programming in C. The aim was not to become a developer, but to gain an inside understanding of the connected systems and embedded electronics I work with in mechanical design, so that I would no longer be dealing with a ‘black box’ in my approach to smart products.
education-text-3: Lyon 1 University of Technology offers a hands-on, practical GMP course covering computer-aided design, manufacturing, automation and production management. The course combines theory, laboratory work and industry placements. I took advantage of an international partnership to spend a semester and undertake an internship in Quebec.
education-text-4: The PTSI course at La Martinière Monplaisir gave me a solid foundation in the basic sciences and engineering: physics, mathematics, engineering sciences and industrial technologies. It is a demanding programme, focused on analysis, critical thinking and the resolution of complex problems.
education-tile-subtitle-1: Product Design and Innovation, work-study programme
hobbies-intro: My hobbies: handcrafting bicycle frames, collecting and reverse engineering, the maker movement, analogue photography and mountain sports.
home-a1-summary: Accelerate the development of an industrial process using EF simulation and design of experiments to ensure quality reliability.
home-a2-summary: Design a high-speed, autonomous sorting machine to eliminate costly production line stoppages.
home-a3-summary: Exploring metal additive manufacturing to reduce the weight and enhance the performance of an aerospace bearing cage.
home-a4-summary: Design and build a bespoke steel frame from start to finish, under the guidance of a frame-building craftsman.
home-a5-summary: Collecting and reverse-engineering historic bicycles to understand the evolution of cycling technology.
home-a6-summary: Taking analog photographs and collecting iconic cameras to understand how they work.
home-edu-2:
INSA Lyon - [2017–2021]
Master’s degree in Mechanical Engineering
home-edu-3-summary: DUT GMP in Mechanical and Production Engineering
home-intro-t1: As a mechanical design engineer, I tackle technical challenges using a rigorous methodology to design innovative products. At NTN-SNR, I led three R&D projects from start to finish : mechatronic design, multiphysics FEA simulation and metal additive manufacturing. A deliberate stint at École 42 and the co-founding of a start-up taught me to think in terms of products as much as individual parts and to communicate seamlessly with technical teams. My bilingual proficiency in English facilitates my integration into an international environment.
home-intro-t2: Based in the Lyon area, I am looking to join a team where my versatility will be an asset as we work together to design ambitious products and systems!
item-1-desc: A source of innovation in mechanical product R&D, from concept to production. Constant monitoring of manufacturing methods and processes.
item-2-desc: Applies the scientific method and experimental designs. Analyses complex data to draw relevant conclusions.
item-3-desc: Expertise in rapid prototyping, topological optimisation and additive manufacturing, to design high-value-added geometries.
item-4-desc: Excellent communication skills, good interpersonal skills, and fluency in English to ensure effective collaboration in an international setting.
item-5-desc: Methods and a holistic approach to successfully delivering multidisciplinary technical projects.
item-6-desc: System programming: low-level C programming for embedded control systems, sensors and actuators, computational and automation tools.

resume-exp-1-b1:
Development of a B2B SaaS solution for film distribution:

→ team coordination, needs analysis, definition of functional specifications, value proposition assessment, UX Design training, web-app prototyping.
resume-exp-2-b1:
Develop a high-throughput autonomous sorting machine for tapered rollers (350 pcs/min, Δ∅ < 3 mm):

→ led end to end: analysis, technology selection, mechanical design and electro-pneumatic circuits, dedicated kinematics, embedded Arduino/C control with Keyence laser sensors, commissioning, on-site testing, process validation and handover to production engineering.
resume-exp-2-b2:
Redesigned an aeronautical engine bearing cage using metal additive manufacturing (MAM):

→ topology optimisation (SolidWorks FE), mass reduction, integration of emergency lubrication channels impossible to machine conventionally, prototyping. MAM exploration to motivate R&D investment in the new process.
resume-exp-2-b3:
Optimised the localised induction heat-treatment (LHT) process:

→ Taguchi design of experiments (mechanics, thermal physics, electromagnetism, materials science), sensitivity analysis of FE simulation parameters, test inductors made by copper AM, test campaigns with Forge NXT editor for solver calibration. Simulation management and data setup, VBA parameterisation tools delivered and in use by the LHT department; development cycle made more efficient.

resume-summary-text: As an INSA Lyon engineer, I led three end-to-end R&D projects at NTN-SNR Roulements: full mechatronic design, multiphysics FE simulation, and additive manufacturing. A deliberate detour through École 42 and co-founding a startup taught me to think product as much as part. I am looking for a product design engineering role within a company building ambitious products.
work-intro: Four projects, ranging from the optimisation of an industrial process to the design of a sorting machine, including the development of an aerospace component using additive manufacturing and the management of a SaaS start-up project.
